Revised Route of Cable
South Arches 235C, D and E under Station. Leak Street East Arch 233B adjacent to Station Milk. Arches 1 & 2, located within Mepham Street Arches.

Proceed from main station concourse via the stairs to the rear of platforms 6/7 signed 'To Waterloo Road and the Old Vic Theatre', at the bottom of these stairs turn right again into Lower Road <which is a railway term and is not signed as such> this will take you to the E.P.S. security gate No.6 where you will have to show your identity card. If you do not have an identity card, you must request the gateman to telephone the Plan Arch to confirm that you are expected. Then proceed to Control gate No.7 which is the exit from Lower Road into Lancelot Street, after passing through a set of gates turn right into a small courtyard. The Plan Arch is the 3rd door on the right <Arch 236D>. Security gates are manned between 07:00 and 19:00. Details of walking routes trackside beyond the boundaries of the station are contained within National Hazard Directory

Limit of 3 tonnes gross vehicle and plant weight limit applicable to all concourse and platform areas. Requests for dispensation up to a higher limit must be accompanied by a structural assessment and submitted to the Senior Asset Manager for consideration.

Limit of 7 tonnes gross vehicle and plant weight limit applicable from the access road. Requests for dispensation up to a higher limit must be accompanied by a structural assessment and submitted to the Senior Asset Manager for consideration.

Limited records of gas supply pipelines are held by the Senior Asset Manager and the Facility Delivery Manager. All mains gas supply pipes up to and including the meter are the responsibility of National Grid. In case of emergency the station has contact details. Natural gas is non-toxic, but large concentrations could cause suffocation. It is highly flammable and leakage from a pipeline could result in an explosive mixture with air. Noise of escaping gas could be sufficient to cause damage to hearing and ear protection must be worn. All persons must be kept 100 metres from the damaged pipeline.

All rail tracks at Waterloo Station are electrified by 750-volt DC electric traction supply cables from track level third rails. All works undertaken on the station must be undertaken in accordance with the Network Rail instructions concerning personal safety on electrified lines.

Requirement to disturb the surface within the station's controlled area, the Buried Services Procedure shall be followed at all times. Work will not be allowed to commence until such time that a Permit to Dig/Penetrate form has been duly signed by the Contract Administrator. It is suspected that low voltage cables are located underground throughout the station. Each contractor will be responsible for the scanning of the work site in order to locate the exact location of the underground power cable
Asbestos hazards please see ARMS for specific details

The Arch wall is covered with plastic corrugated sheeting and care must be taken in the event of cutting or undertaking any hot works, for which a permit is required as the Plan Arches are `No smoking? areas . Within this arch is a panel in the floor which may be access to sewer or drain. Air conditioning equipment is provided within this and arches 233B, 234, 236, 236D, 236E.The hazards associated with equipment are mainly electrical, chemical and biological. When accessing the mezzanine floor, especially with tools or equipment, care must be taken to avoid damage to the air conditioning equipment.

Arch wall is covered with plastic corrugated sheeting and care must be taken in the event of cutting or undertaking any hot works, for which a permit is required as the Plan Arches are `No smoking? areas. 33kv cables are contained in troughing suspended from the arch roof.When accessing the mezzanine floor, especially with tools or equipment, care must be taken to avoid damage to the air conditioning equipment.

Arch wall is covered with plastic corrugated sheeting and care must be taken in the event of cutting or undertaking any hot works, for which a permit is required as the Plan Arches are `No smoking? areas.The fire bottles for the sprinkler system are contained within this arch. There is also an electrical distribution board.

Arch wall is covered with plastic corrugated sheeting and care must be taken in the event of cutting or undertaking any hot works, for which a permit is required as the Plan Arches are `No smoking? areas.There is also an electrical distribution board and printing equipment located within this arch.

Arch wall is covered with plastic corrugated sheeting and care must be taken in the event of cutting or undertaking any hot works, for which a permit is required as the Plan Arches are `No smoking? areas.There is also an electrical distribution board contained within a small purpose built brick enclosure.The arch also contains office accommodation and toilets. The normal hazards are to be found in these areas e.g. electrical, water etc. Exits lead to arches leased by W H Smith and South West Trains.

The roof space above the toilet area contains the gas boiler and water tanks associated with the hot and cold water system. Access to this area is via a pull down loft ladder. The layout and positioning of the equipment in this area could make the removal of an injured person difficult; this area should be considered as a confined space.The main hazards to be considered are gas, electrical, water, access/egress to roof space.

The main hazard presented by this is that of fire, especially where the integrity of the electrical circuits is not known. A number of the pipes from the mains supply of cold water form long dead legs in which the water may be stagnant or contaminated. In addition to the domestic and mains water there are routed through the arches water pipes associated with fire systems. Many pipes and cable runs are in the roof of the arches. the use of ladders or portable access towers with the associated risk of falls.

A number of the pipes from the mains supply of cold water form long dead legs in which the water may be stagnant or contaminated. Also subject to sewage flooding. toilet facilities have been removed and waste pipes have been left uncapped or the caps have become damaged. The floors of the arches vary considerably with uneven and bare earth/rubble. Numerous tripping hazards are presented by the floor conditions, waste materials, damaged drain covers. Many pipes and cable runs are in the roof of the arches. the use of ladders or portable access towers with the associated risk of falls.The rear area of the arch contains a caged area used for hazardous substances chemicals. Lagged pipes run through parts of the arch. lagging material is that of fibreglass and not verified.

Access to confined spaces/limited access sewers and space at rear of pump room. the use of internal combustion engined plant, LPG. Many of the pipe and cable runs are in the roof of the arches. the use of ladders and portable access towers with the associated risk of falls. Numerous tripping hazards are presented by the floor conditions uneven and bare earth/rubble, waste materials, damaged drain covers.

Domestic water supplies some are capped and form `dead legs?. At the rear of door `28? <off the subway to Waterloo & City> and adjacent to the entrance to an office there is a manhole in the floor. The passageway at the `Lower Road? entrance to the arch has a low tiled ceiling. From initial examination it could not be ascertained if the tiles are asbestos. A small `room? immediately inside and to the left of the entrance door has a manhole cover in the floor which is just a drain cover.

Within the switch room, mess room and male toilets there are access panels in the floor giving access to cable runs. This void beneath the raft should be considered as a confined space.The panelling material used in the construction of the raft contains asbestos <signs are affixed at regular intervals to warn of the hazard>. The stairwell at platform 1/2 end of `B? Block contains a cupboard containing electrical equipment and ducting. This may be considered as a confined space due to its sitting and access. On the roof area of Block `A? is sited an air conditioning unit. The hazards associated with work on the raft are falls from height, electrical, gas, hazardous substances, biological, confined space/limited access.

Contains boilers and pumps associated with the main hot water system. Some of the equipment appears to be decommissioned not readily identifiable. There are manholes/drain covers in the floor which could not be opened to ascertain their depth etc. These should in the first instance be classified as confined spaces, subject to detailed survey.The roof of the boiler house is translucent and should be considered as fragile. Access to much of the pipework, valves etc. requires working at height. There is a large diameter mains gas supply in the boiler house feeding the operational boilers; also electrical control equipment. The majority of pipework would appear to be lagged with fibreglass insulation; however it is suspected that some lagging is asbestos.The brick chimney used prior to the installation of the gas-fired boilers should be classified as a confined space if any work is required on its interior.

The sub-station is located on the extremity of the station boundary adjacent to EPS owned property and the Network Rail Plan Store.The entrance way is a narrow passage accessed from the pavement on Leake Street. The sub-station consists of a high voltage and low voltage room.Suspended from the roof of the sub-station there are ducts associated with dust extraction/air conditioning. The ducting is fitted with access panels. The ducting would be classified as a confined space for anyone required to work inside.The exit from the high voltage room is into EPS property whilst the exits from the low voltage room are into the access corridor, off which is a further exit into the Plan Network Rail Store. The main hazards associated with the sub-station are electrical, fire and falls from height.

The sub-station consists of a high voltage and low voltage room. This could become a confined space. Suspended from the roof of the sub-station there are ducts associated with dust extraction/air conditioning. The ducting is fitted with access panels. The ducting would be classified as a confined space for anyone required to work inside.The main hazards associated with the sub-station are electrical, fire and falls from height.

The sub-station is located within the area now owned by EPS. The sub-station consists of a high voltage i.e. 11kv and a low voltage room. Some equipment within the rooms are filled with SF6.The sub-station has a number of alarmed exit doors. Suspended from the roof of the sub-station there are ducts associated with dust extraction/air conditioning. The ducting is fitted with access panels. The ducting would be classified as a confined space for anyone required to work inside.The main hazards associated with the sub-station are electrical, hazardous substance, fire and falls from height.

There are also two plant rooms. One contains a boiler the other an air-cooling unit. Each of the plant rooms has an internal `bund wall? approximately 4? high which presents a tripping hazard at the entrance doors.The boiler is gas fed and both rooms contain electrical control equipment and plant.There are numerous pipes, ducting and electrical cables suspended from the ceiling of the raft. Work in this area is hampered by the space available and its access. May need to be considered as a confined space.The raft is constructed above and spans platforms 1 to 10. Access is via spiral staircases contained within glass-enclosed wells. Work on the external fabric of the structure would require scaffold etc. Access to the roof of the raft is via vertical fixed ladders <one at each end> from the accommodation level. There is no evidence of panels containing asbestos.The main hazards associated with this area are electrical, gas, water, limited space, falls from height, tripping.

This subway has monitors suspended from the ceiling at the foot of the stairs leading down from the platforms. There are small voids in the `roof? space above these areas. Electric cables run the length of the subway and serve a number of displays.The floor of the subway could become slippery in the event of minor flooding.
This room contains electrical equipment.
This room contains electrical equipment.
This room contains electrical equipment.
Fire Valve Room contains electrical equipment, water supply and valves associated with the fire system.

Limited records exist of drainage, electrical and water supply pipelines held in Station Reception, and with Senior Asset Manager and the Facility Delivery Manager. Contractors should note that these would be present to most areas of the station, including platforms.
Vent Fan Room. This room contains electrical equipment and small ducting which is suspended from the ceiling.

The platforms have voids beneath containing electrical cables, drains etc. <especially 2 ? 13>. live electrical wiring and flexible 25mm water supply pipes are present on the floors. Numerous gaps & holes in the void floor which could lead to falls into the arches below. Extreme care required including the use of lighting when walking through these voids. Access to these voids is via manholes at the country and concourse ends of the platforms.Hinged lighting standards are sited on those areas of the platforms not covered by the train-shed roof. Platform 11/12 has a compound used by Initial for the charging of electric vehicles and also the storage of chemicals etc used in connection with station cleaning. This platform also has ramped access to the EPS owned arches beneath.At the barrier end of the platform is a large electrical equipment cabinet containing high voltage equipment.

Within the `roof space? of this small room run ducts and electrical cables; access to these is limited.The normal hazards associated with these areas are electrical, hazardous substances and biological hazards. In particular, the danger of needle stick injuries from used hypodermic syringes can be present

The run contains numerous pipe and cable runs and electrical equipment. Many of the runs cross each other making access difficult. This area should be considered as a confined space. From the main lobby towards arch 291 and the toilet lobby exit the rat run is tall and narrow with cable and pipe runs along the walls. Approximately at the midway point, and set back in a small alcove to the left, is an opening in the floor. This, and the void beneath should be considered as a confined space if it is necessary for persons to enter. Further beyond, there is a further opening below floor level into which passes heating pipes <the lagging of which would appear to be glass fibre based>. this opening should be considered as a confined space.
Adjacent to arch 233B there is an over hanging girder-forming part of a hoist located within the maintenance engineers yard. This girder extends over the full width of the pavement.

From the public highway the road is bounded on both sides by brick walls topped by a combination of iron railings <some spiked> and steel expanded wire mesh.Access to LUL Waterloo & City line underground workshop area is covered by large hinged `trap doors? of expanded steel mesh construction. At the northwestern corner of the access is a 2 tonne electric hoist which is permanently erected.The road has a number of illuminated traffic signs and street lighting standards which present electrical hazards. Public parking of road vehicles is allowed on both sides of Spur Road.The main hazards associated with work in this area are road vehicles, electrical, working at height.

Work on the boundary structure running along side of this short no through road would mainly be at height. The lower part of the structure i.e. arch openings are steel lattice with spike tops. In addition there is the hazard of illegally parked road vehicles.

two plant rooms contains the pump and other hydraulic equipment associated with the operation of the disabled persons lift giving access to Waterloo East. The hazards presented in this area are electrical; hazardous substances i.e. hydraulic oil; pressure system i.e. oil pump and associated hoses/pipes etc; high ambient temperature in the room. It may be appropriate to consider this as a confined space.The second plant room includes electrical equipment <of differing voltages>, water heating pipes, ducting etc.Some of the equipment is mounted on a permanent steel framework, contained within the plant room and reached by a fixed vertical ladder, whilst other items are fixed to lightweight tubular steel to which is fixed the suspended ceiling above the office area. Due to the access to certain parts of this room it should be considered as a confined space/limited access.

Disabled Persons Lift is located at the foot of the public subway steps. This room is approximately 8? x 8? and contains the pump and other hydraulic equipment associated with the operation of the lift. The hazards presented in this area are electrical; hazardous substances i.e. hydraulic oil; pressure system i.e. oil pump and associated hoses/pipes etc; high ambient temperature in the room. If, for security purposes, it is a requirement to undertake works in this room with the door closed then the temperature would be excessive. It would be appropriate to consider this as a confined space.

Station entrance via escalator access to the lower machine rooms is via staircases in the corridor off the Colonnade lift lobby. The lower machine room areas are free of extraneous materials. Due to their location and access it is considered appropriate to classify them as confined spaces. The main hazards within these areas are electrical. One room has a water supply. It was noted that moving parts of the escalator are protected by fixed guards.

This area is a tenanted to non-railway commercial tenants. Network Rail retains a landlord interest in the premises and may, on occasion, be called upon to carry out works within it. Amosite and chrysotile is known to be present on both the ground and first floors in cladding and ceiling panels. Relevant items are presently in a safe condition and have been labelled as such by the tenants. Works should not to be carried out to these areas without expert advice.

All operatives working must be aware of the limits of their working areas due to hazards associated with the presence of multiple substations and 750V DC feeding arrangements.~
~
The Up/Down Arrivals Road and Feltham Depot tracks are electrically fed from East Feltham Substation and are located London end of Feltham Station on the Down Cess.~
The Up and Down Main RDG1 tracks are electrically fed from Feltham Substation which is located on the Up Cess.~
~
The Up/Down Arrivals Road is parallel to the Up and Down Main RDG1 and connects at both London & Country End. Operatives working in this area must be aware that the conductor rail system on the Up/Down Arrivals Road and connections to the Down Main RDG1 and Feltham Depot may still be live if the Up and Down Main RDG1 is under isolation through Feltham Substation. Similarly where an isolation has been taken on the Up/Down Arrivals Road through East Feltham Substation, the depot, Up and Down Main RDG1 and connections may still be live.

Awareness of hazards associated with bi-directional working/unpredictable train movements within the Feltham Stn & Depot area.~
The UP/DN Arrivals Road is a bi-directional line, primarily for access to Feltham Depot, also as a passing loop for the RDG1 & facilitating turnback moves. ~
The UP/DN Arrivals Road can accommodate trains at the London & Country end of 675 pts simultaneously. Sections of bi-directional working are permitted on the UP & DN Main RDG1for accessing the UP/DN Arrivals Road. Trains can access the UP/DN Arrivals Road from London & Country End <line speed 15mph> & from Feltham Depot via 675 points <5mph>. Trains accessing the UP/DN Arrivals Road may stop at signals & reverse in the opposite direction.Bidirectional working is permitted on the DN Main to access the UP/DN Arrivals Road from signal F2154 at Feltham Stn P?form 2 & on the UP Main, to access the UP/DN Arrivals Road from F453. A bi di move up to signal F3159 on UP Main at Feltham Stn P?form 1 is also possible

Two driver"s hop-ups are provided on the Up Line. The first is located at approximately Ch.29873 <18m 990yds> and the other hop-up is located at the Up Side siding buffer stop at approximately Ch.30381 <18m 1545yds>. The primary purpose of the Haliford end hop-up is to facilitate a driver changing ends of a 10 car set in order to perform a Z-shunt move back into the Up Siding. The Up Siding buffer stop end hop-up is to allow drivers access on and off the cab from the walking route. These structures are of Glass Reinforced Plastic with the upper ?platform? level being accessed by stairs. Handrails are provided to the back of and over the full length of the structures <i.e. including the stairs> to prevent falls.

There is a formal walking route up to the redundant platform with dedicated lighting. Lighting for the Haliford hop-up is provided but there is no formal walking route on the Up cess beyond the redundant platform from Ch.30268 <18m 1421yds> to the Haliford hop-up. The supply cables to the luminaires are formally managed in troughing and do not present a hazard to pedestrian traffic. Their presence however, is to be noted for engineering and maintenance activities.

The Staines Down Goods Sidings must be marked "Out of Use in the Safe System Of Work Pack <SSOWP>" for any future engineering works. The Staines Down Goods Loop has been plain lined and removed from service as part of Feltham resignalling phase 3&4 and is therefore no longer available and is not to be used.

The Planned Crossing Point (PCP) is for the use of Network Rail staff only (during the trial phase).
A PCP can only be used by an IWA or a COSS with a group of 3 additional staff (maximum 4 people)
A PCP cannot be used during darkness or poor visibility that affects the sighting of trains.
If an IWA, COSS or group observe a train prior to using the PCP, they shall not cross the open lines until there are no train(s) in sight in either direction.
Only items that can be carried in one hand or stowed in a rucksack can be carried across a PCP.
PCP's may only be used as part of a planned SSOW.

From approximately Ch.99300 <61m 58.7ch> to approximately Ch.99669 <61m 77.1ch> adjacent to the 10-foot rail between the siding and the DN Main.~
From approximately Ch.99671 <61m 77.2ch> to approximately Ch.99697 <61m 78.5ch> adjacent to the cess alongside the diverging lead rail at 25A Points

This a formal walking route with dedicated lighting. The supply cables to the luminaires are formally managed in troughing and do not present a hazard to pedestrian traffic. The primary purpose of the walking route is to allow train drivers access to/from rolling stock temporarily stabled in the siding. These structures are of Glass Reinforced Plastic with the upper ?platform? level being accessed by stairs. Handrails are provided to the back of and over the full length of the structures <i.e. including the stairs> to prevent falls.
